name: GPT-5.4 mini
archDisplay: Undisclosed architecture (presumed dense transformer); cost-efficient mid-tier sibling of GPT-5.4, optimized for coding, computer use, and subagent workloads, running more than 2x faster than the flagship
note: Mid-tier sibling of GPT-5.4, launched Mar 17 2026 (12 days after the flagship/Pro) via OpenAI's own 'Introducing GPT-5.4 mini and nano' announcement as a distinct, separately-priced, separately-benchmarked model -- not a rename or repricing of GPT-5 mini. Runs >2x faster than GPT-5 mini and beats it on every published coding/tool/knowledge/vision benchmark; available in ChatGPT (Free/Go default, fallback for others), Codex, and the API.

results.notes: SWE-Bench Pro (Public), xhigh reasoning effort. Flagship scores 57.7%, GPT-5 mini scores 45.7% on the same table. Cross-confirmed verbatim by datacamp.com/blog/gpt-5-4-mini-nano (independent secondary source).
Terminal-Bench 2.0, xhigh reasoning effort. Cross-confirmed verbatim by datacamp.com/blog/gpt-5-4-mini-nano.
OSWorld-Verified, xhigh reasoning effort. Cross-confirmed verbatim by datacamp.com/blog/gpt-5-4-mini-nano.
Humanity's Last Exam, no tools, xhigh reasoning effort (with-tools variant scores 41.5%).
MMMU-Pro (standard, no Python tool; the 'w/ Python' variant scores 78.0%), xhigh reasoning effort.
ARC-AGI-2 from Epoch AI's independent evaluation of GPT-5.4 mini, relayed via themodelbeat.com.
FrontierMath (Tiers 1-3) from Epoch AI's independent evaluation of GPT-5.4 mini, relayed via themodelbeat.com.
FrontierMath Tier 4 (hardest tier) from Epoch AI's independent evaluation of GPT-5.4 mini, relayed via themodelbeat.com.

AIME 2024/2025 (combined) from Epoch AI's independent evaluation of GPT-5.4 mini, relayed via themodelbeat.com. Note: a separate low-quality source (layerlens.ai) reported an implausible AIME figure of 6.67% for this model (lower than its own HLE score, and wildly inconsistent with peer models) -- that figure was checked and rejected as unreliable rather than used here.

OpenAI's own launch post reports 57.7% on MCP Atlas (quoted verbatim by three independent outlets: 'On MCP Atlas, mini reaches 57.7% versus 47.6% for GPT-5 mini'). Independently corroborated by Scale AI's official MCP Atlas leaderboard, which lists 'gpt-5.4-mini (xhigh) — 56.70±3.10', closely matching.
Model identity confirmed via matching release date (3/17/2026), 400k context, 128k max output, and $0.75/$4.5 pricing on the same vals.ai page.
Same model-identity confirmation as the livecodebench addition.

verificationNotes:
Existence and distinctness from GPT-5 mini confirmed by directly fetching OpenAI's own official launch post (openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-4-mini-and-nano/, dated Mar 17 2026), which explicitly benchmarks GPT-5.4 mini against GPT-5 mini side by side and states it 'significantly improves over GPT-5 mini across coding, reasoning, multimodal understanding, and tool use.' Pricing ($0.75/$4.50 per M), 400K context, and 128K max output independently confirmed via OpenAI's live developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4-mini model card (own snapshot dated gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17, matching the announcement date). Independent secondary source datacamp.com/blog/gpt-5-4-mini-nano (fetched directly) corroborates the same benchmark table verbatim and its FAQ states plainly: 'Is GPT-5.4 mini just a faster GPT-5 mini? No, it's both faster and significantly stronger on benchmarks... while keeping a 400k context window.' All 7 result rows taken from OpenAI's own multi-table benchmark breakdown (Coding/Tool-calling/Intelligence/MM-Vision-CUA sections); Toolathlon and MCP Atlas scores were also published but omitted here since no corpus benchmark slug matches them.

2026-07-16: Researched the 6 flagged core-benchmark gaps (mmlu, math, humaneval, livecodebench, swe-bench, mmmu) across OpenAI's own launch materials, independent leaderboards, and sibling-model cross-reference. OpenAI's own launch table was re-confirmed complete via the-decoder.com's independent full re-listing of the same table (openai.com itself returns HTTP 403 to automated fetches) -- it contains no MMLU, MATH, HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, SWE-bench (original), or MMMU (original) rows for GPT-5.4 mini. Checked Epoch AI (themodelbeat.com/models/gpt-5-4-mini), Artificial Analysis (artificialanalysis.ai/models/gpt-5-4-mini), DataCamp, BenchLM.ai, and lmmarketcap.com's dedicated MMLU leaderboard (which lists the GPT-5.4 flagship at 94% but has no GPT-5.4 mini entry at all) -- none publish MMLU, MATH, or a true (non-Pro) MMMU score for this model either. Vals.ai's live-rendered benchmark suite (fetched in-browser since WebFetch's markdown conversion drops its chart values) did yield two real, citable, independent scores that were added: LiveCodeBench 81.47% and SWE-bench Verified 73.00%, both from vals.ai/models/openai_gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17, whose release date, context window, and pricing all match this catalog's spec fields exactly, confirming correct model identity. Vals.ai's own "/benchmarks/mmmu" leaderboard turned out, on inspection of its page title and dataset description, to actually be MMMU-Pro under the hood -- so the 79.25% "MMMU" figure shown on GPT-5.4 mini's vals.ai page is really an independent MMMU-Pro measurement (a different corpus slug, already populated from OpenAI's own 76.6% self-report) and was correctly excluded rather than misfiled under the plain mmmu slug. The only source claiming an MMLU-Pro (55.29%) and MATH-500 (86.6%) figure for this model is layerlens.ai -- the same low-quality aggregator already flagged and rejected elsewhere in this file for fabricating an implausible AIME score of 6.67%; re-fetching it now reproduces that identical wrong 6.67% AIME figure plus a Humanity's Last Exam figure (5.56%) that flatly contradicts this file's well-sourced self-reported HLE score (28.2%), reconfirming the source as unreliable, so its MMLU-Pro/MATH-500 numbers were rejected too (and MMLU-Pro would be a different slug regardless). Sibling models GPT-5.4 (flagship) and GPT-5.4 nano also carry no mmlu/math/humaneval results anywhere in this corpus, consistent with OpenAI no longer publishing these saturated legacy benchmarks for the GPT-5.4 family -- mmlu, math, humaneval, and mmmu (true, non-Pro variant) are accordingly treated as genuinely unpublished for GPT-5.4 mini rather than guessed at.

2026-07-18: Re-chased the flagged 'mmmu' (plain, non-Pro) gap specifically. First confirmed applicability: this model's own corpus record lists modalities ["text","vision"] and capabilities.vision=true, so MMMU is architecturally applicable (not a text-only model) -- any absence must be treated as "not yet found," not "doesn't exist," per the task's instructions. Checked 6 of 6 research methods: (1) OpenAI's own launch post (openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-4-mini-and-nano/, and re-fetched openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-4/ -- both 403 to automated fetch, consistent with prior rounds) plus its full verbatim re-listing via the-decoder.com and datacamp.com -- multimodal section contains only MMMU-Pro (76.6% / 78.0% w/ Python), no plain MMMU row. (2) Independent trackers: Epoch AI's full eval table (themodelbeat.com/models/gpt-5-4-mini, re-fetched fresh) lists ARC-AGI, ARC-AGI-2, GPQA Diamond, HLE, SimpleQA Verified, WeirdML, SciCode, WebDev Arena, AIME, FrontierMath (both tiers), APEX, and tau2-bench -- no MMMU of any kind. Artificial Analysis (artificialanalysis.ai/models/gpt-5-4-mini) lists MMMU-Pro under its 'visual reasoning' category with no accompanying score and no separate plain-MMMU entry. BenchLM.ai's per-model page (benchlm.ai/models/gpt-5-4-mini) explicitly lists a full Multimodal category containing only 'MMMU-Pro: 76.6%' and 'MMMU-Pro with Python: 78%', with an explicit note that no separate plain MMMU result is documented. llm-stats.com's dedicated page has an MMMU nav entry but zero populated scores for this model (page is a stub: 'Scores sourced from the model's scorecard... notice missing or incorrect data?'). Vals.ai's page titled 'MMMU Pro' (vals.ai/benchmarks/mmmu, re-confirmed via search) is, per its own title, MMMU-Pro under the plain-sounding URL slug -- already correctly excluded in a prior round as a mislabeled duplicate of the mmmu-pro slug already in this file. (3) Academic: targeted arXiv searches for "gpt-5.4-mini" MMMU and site:arxiv.org queries returned no papers using this exact model as an MMMU baseline. (4) GitHub: searched openai/simple-evals and MMMU-Benchmark/MMMU repos plus general GitHub code/log search for gpt-5.4-mini MMMU result files -- none found; also checked the MMMU benchmark's own official leaderboard (mmmu-benchmark.github.io), which is stale and lists no GPT-5 series model at all (newest entry is GPT-4V), confirming it is not an active source for this model either way. (5) Sibling-model cross-reference: GPT-5.4 nano's own benchmark pages (benchlm.ai/models/gpt-5-4-nano, llm-stats.com/models/gpt-5.4-nano) and GPT-5.5-vs-GPT-5.4 comparison writeups (llm-stats.com/blog/research/gpt-5-5-vs-gpt-5.4) cite only MMMU-Pro figures for the whole GPT-5.4 family, never plain MMMU, reinforcing that OpenAI simply did not publish a plain-MMMU number for this generation. (6) OpenRouter's page (openrouter.ai/openai/gpt-5.4-mini) renders benchmarks client-side and returned no static score data via fetch. Conclusion: every source that discusses GPT-5.4 mini's multimodal/vision performance -- OpenAI's own materials, all five independent trackers checked, and every sibling-model launch comparison -- reports MMMU-Pro and only MMMU-Pro; none publish a plain (non-Pro) MMMU score for this model. This is consistent with the 2026-07-16 note's finding and is now reconfirmed via a fresh, independent second pass: treated as genuinely unpublished (OpenAI appears to have replaced plain MMMU with MMMU-Pro as its standard multimodal benchmark for the GPT-5.4 family), not as a search failure, so no 'mmmu' row is added and the gap remains an intentional omission rather than a fabricated estimate.
